task: I’m putting together a short note for my team before a busy stretch, and I want it to be clear enough that everyone knows what to focus on first. Can you help me draft something that sets the agenda for the week and keeps the message straightforward?

prefs.explanation: He is driven by results and status, so he likely prefers help that gets to the point quickly. A softened or overly diplomatic style would probably feel inefficient to him. He would respond better to direct wording that makes the action or issue immediately clear.

prefs.short: Prefers concise answers over long explanations
prefs.explanation: His work style is efficiency-focused, and he likely values responses that save time rather than unpack everything. Long-winded help would feel like drag unless the extra detail clearly affects the outcome. He probably wants the shortest answer that still lets him act decisively.

prefs.short: Likes responses in clean bullet points or checklists
prefs.explanation: He seems to prefer information that can be scanned and used fast, especially in a managerial setting. A structured format fits his control-oriented style and reduces ambiguity. He would likely dislike rambling prose when a simple checklist would do.

prefs.short: Can tolerate firm pushback if it protects results
prefs.explanation: He is not looking for emotional cushioning, so a direct warning about risk would likely be acceptable if it is practical. Because he has already been burned by compliance problems, he may respect pushback that prevents mistakes. He would still resist pushback that feels like moralizing or slows execution without clear benefit.

prefs.short: Prefers plain English over fancy wording
prefs.explanation: He seems likely to value clear, practical language that reads like business communication rather than polished rhetoric. Jargon is fine only if it is useful and common in the setting. Overly formal or literary phrasing would probably feel unnecessary to him.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — keep it short and direct.

## Simple team note
“Team, we have a busy week ahead, so I want us focused on the items that matter most. First priority is anything due today or tied to a deadline. Next is anything waiting on someone else that could slow us down. After that, we’ll handle the items that carry risk or could create cleanup later. Please keep follow-ups visible, keep owners clear, and flag anything that may slip before it becomes a problem.”

## Slightly firmer version
“Team, this week is going to move fast, so we need to stay on top of the work that matters. Handle today’s deadlines first, then anything blocked by someone else, then anything that could create risk or rework. Keep your follow-ups updated, don’t let items sit without an owner, and speak up early if something is starting to drift.”

## Short closing line
“Let’s keep the week tight, stay ahead of the work, and avoid cleanup later.”

If you want, I can make it sound more like your branch voice — firmer, more direct, or more conversational.

per_pref.reason: Very direct and blunt: “keep it short and direct,” “First priority,” “don’t let items sit without an owner,” and “speak up early if something is starting to drift.”
Mostly concise and action-focused, with a short note plus two variants. Slightly more than minimal because it offers multiple versions, but still efficient.
Uses clean structure with headings and a checklist-like priority order: “First priority… Next… After that…” and separate sections for versions and closing line.
Includes firm pushback/risk framing without moralizing: “anything that could create risk or rework,” “flag anything that may slip before it becomes a problem,” and “avoid cleanup later.”
Plain English throughout, with practical wording like “busy week ahead,” “blocked by someone else,” and “keep your follow-ups updated,” avoiding fancy or literary phrasing.
